title: Cestrum lewisii
taxonomy: Cestrum lewisii is a species of flowering plant in the nightshade family Solanaceae. It belongs to the genus Cestrum, which comprises around 100 species of tropical plants. The family Solanaceae includes a wide range of plants, such as tomatoes, potatoes, and eggplants. Cestrum lewisii is native to tropical regions and is known for its clusters of small, funnel-shaped flowers. The exact classification of Cestrum lewisii may be subject to ongoing research and updates in botanical classification.

side_effects: Cestrum lewisii, a plant native to Central and South America, contains toxic compounds, including solanine and scopolamine. Ingestion of the plant's berries, flowers, or leaves can cause gastrointestinal symptoms, such as vomiting and diarrhea, in animals and humans. The plant's toxic compounds can also cause more severe symptoms, such as hallucinations and increased heart rate, in larger doses. In rare cases, ingestion of the plant can lead to more serious health issues, including respiratory failure and cardiac arrest. It is essential to handle Cestrum lewisii with caution and avoid ingesting any parts of the plant.
intro: Cestrum lewisii, also known as the night-blooming cestrum or the night-blooming chandelier flower, is a flowering plant native to the Andean regions of South America. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can aid in relieving symptoms of fever, respiratory issues, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Cestrum lewisii include alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Cestrum lewisii include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat various health conditions in traditional medicine.
